note: Header row. Real records start after first REFRESH. Schema: id, source_ids, claim, observed_at (ISO8601), dossier, weight (0-1)

claim: LangChain 1.0 and LangGraph 1.0 GA released on Oct 22, 2025. No breaking changes committed until 2.0. 90M monthly downloads; used in production at Uber, LinkedIn, Klarna, Rippling, JP Morgan, Blackrock, Cisco.

create_agent is the new 1.0 standard entry point in LangChain, built on LangGraph runtime. AgentExecutor is deprecated; create_react_agent in langgraph.prebuilt is superseded. Migration is a few-line drop-in for simple ReAct loops, a rewrite into StateGraph for non-trivial cases.

markdown-it is a mature, plugin-based JavaScript markdown parser. 100% Markdown 1.0, 100% CommonMark 0.31, full GFM. Plugin system: emoji, subscript, superscript, ins, mark, footnotes, definition lists, abbreviations, custom containers (::: classname via markdown-it-container), markdown-it-anchor for auto-generated heading IDs. Token-stream architecture with comprehensive renderer customization. Configurable html / xhtmlOut / breaks / linkify / typographer / highlight. CommonMark strict toggle.

markdown-it-container plugin enables Pandoc-style fenced divs: '::: classname' on a line, content on subsequent lines, ':::' to close. This is the portable way to add render hints (callout, fade-in-stagger, collapse, source-trail) to markdown without inventing a new dialect.

markdown-it has zero required runtime dependencies beyond its core. AST is exposed via tokens. Used by Vue.js docs, VuePress, VitePress, Astro, Logseq as the default markdown pipeline. Mature since 2014.

marked.js is the fastest low-level markdown parser. 100% Markdown 1.0, 98% CommonMark 0.31, 97% GFM 0.29. CLI and in-browser. Zero dependencies. Used by zero-md, texme, StrapDown.js, raito, Homebrewery, marked_reader.

marked does NOT sanitize output HTML. The docs explicitly warn: 'If you are processing potentially unsafe strings, it's important to filter for possible XSS attacks. Some filtering options include DOMPurify (recommended), js-xss, sanitize-html and insane on the output HTML.' For trusted-author case (Mavis's vault), sanitization is a recommended discipline, not strictly required.

Pandoc is a universal document converter. 40+ input formats including gfm, commonmark, commonmark_x, markdown, markdown_strict, markdown_phpextra, markdown_mmd, html, latex, docx, rst, etc. -f/--from and -t/--to specify formats.

Pandoc --embed-resources: 'Produce a standalone HTML file with no external dependencies, using data: URIs to incorporate the contents of linked scripts, stylesheets, images, and videos. The resulting file should be self-contained, in the sense that it needs no external files and no net access to be displayed properly by a browser.' This is exactly the single-file-in-the-vault pattern for Mavis's dossiers.
Pandoc --self-contained is the deprecated synonym for --embed-resources --standalone. Modern usage should prefer --embed-resources. -s/--standalone produces valid HTML with <head> and <body>.

GFM (GitHub Flavored Markdown) Spec 0.29 is a strict superset of CommonMark. Extensions over CommonMark: tables, task list items, strikethrough, autolinks, disallowed raw HTML. All user content on github.com uses GFM.

CommonMark block elements: ATX headings (1-6 #), Setext headings (=== or ---), indented code blocks (4 spaces), fenced code blocks (3+ backticks or tildes with optional info string for language), blockquotes, ordered/unordered lists with various markers, link reference definitions, paragraphs, blank lines. Inlines: emphasis, strong emphasis, code spans, links, images, autolinks, raw HTML, hard/soft line breaks. Created by John MacFarlane (jgm), 2014. 600+ conformance tests.
GFM table extension: pipe-delimited columns, header row, optional alignment row (--- | :---: | :---). Renders as <table> with <thead> and <tbody>. markdown-it and pandoc both implement GFM tables.

Quartz 5.0.0 (May 24, 2026) is the leading Obsidian-native static site generator. Features: Obsidian compatibility, full-text search, graph view, wikilinks, transclusions, Backlinks, Latex, syntax highlighting, popover previews, Docker, i18n, comments. Requires Node v22 + npm v10.9.2. Hot-reload, incremental rebuilds. SPA routing. 'Ridiculously fast page loads and tiny bundle sizes.'

Quartz is too heavyweight for Mavis's v1 use case. It produces dozens of pages from a vault; we need ONE page per dossier. Borrow its rendering shapes (popover previews, wikilinks, syntax highlighting) for the dossier HTML; don't pull in Quartz itself.

Material for MkDocs (squidfunk) is a Material Design-flavored static site generator. Just Markdown, works on all devices, customizable, fast and lightweight, MIT, 60+ languages. Built-in instant search (browser-side, no crawler), code annotations, social cards, 10,000+ icons/emojis. Trusted by NASA, CERN, TC39, W3C, Google, Microsoft, Mozilla, Font Awesome, Apache, freeCodeCamp.

Material for MkDocs is Python (not Node), which is friction for Mavis's Node-heavy stack. Skip for v1; if Mavis later wants full-site generation, MkDocs Material is a strong candidate. v1 doesn't need full-site generation; one-dossier-at-a-time is enough.

Eleventy (11ty) v3.1.6 is the canonical 'simpler static site generator.' Build benchmark: 4000 Markdown files in Eleventy 1.93s vs Astro 22.90s, Gatsby 29.05s, Next.js 70.65s. 18.7M downloads, 82K+ dependent repos. Zero client-side JavaScript by default. No telemetry. Used by Chrome for Developers docs.

Eleventy supports 15+ template languages (HTML, Markdown, WebC, JS, Liquid, Nunjucks, Handlebars, Mustache, EJS, Haml, Pug, TypeScript, JSX, MDX, Sass). Independent template languages; not held hostage to a custom format. The 'fast builds, fast websites' pairing makes it the right call for the 'multiple-dossiers-over-time' scale. v2 candidate if Mavis wants full-site generation.

Astro Content Collections (added in astro@2.0.0). Two types: build-time and live. Loaders: glob() (for .md, .mdx, .markdoc, .json, .yaml, .toml directories) and file() (single files). Zod schema validation with TypeScript type-safety. astro:content runtime API: getCollection(), getEntry(), render().

Astro's islands architecture means: zero JS by default, opt-in to JS for interactive components. View transitions built in (Astro's own implementation, not the browser API). Strong candidate for v2 if Mavis wants a content site. For v1, too heavy.

CSS scroll-driven animations module (W3C/CSSWG, MDN canonical). Two timelines: scroll progress timeline (animation tied to scroll position of a scroll container) and view progress timeline (animation tied to an element's relative position in its scroll container). Properties: animation-timeline, scroll-timeline, view-timeline, animation-range. Functions: scroll(), view(). Interfaces: ScrollTimeline, ViewTimeline. Chrome 115+ (Jul 2023); Safari 26; Firefox 'behind a flag' (not yet Baseline).

Scroll-driven animations run OFF the main thread on the compositor. 'You can now have silky smooth animations, driven by scroll, running off the main thread, with just a few lines of extra code.' (Bramus, Chrome DevRel, May 2023).

View Progress Timeline can be paired with animation-range to control which part of the scroll-into-view transition the animation runs over: cover, entry, exit, entry-crossing, exit-crossing, contain. Common pattern: 'animation-range: entry 25% cover 50%' fades content in during the first 25%-50% of the entry phase.

Cross-browser support for animation-timeline is Chrome 115+ only as of 2026-06. Firefox and Safari do not implement it. For Mavis's v1, this means the scroll-driven path is progressive enhancement for Chrome users; Safari/Firefox users get the IntersectionObserver fallback.
Bramus's chrome devrel blog is the canonical explainer for scroll-driven animations. It includes the demo gallery at scroll-driven-animations.style and is the de-facto standard reference.

Scroll-driven animations cover TWO use cases relevant to Mavis: (a) reading progress indicator (scroll-timeline bound to root scroller), (b) image/element fade-in on scroll (view-timeline bound to element). For Mavis's dossier, the second is the primary use case.

IntersectionObserver API: 'Baseline Widely available. Available across browsers since March 2019.' 'Asynchronously observes changes in the intersection of a target element with an ancestor element or with a top-level document's viewport.' Solves the 'fade in on scroll' pattern in 30 lines of vanilla JS, replacing the legacy getBoundingClientRect + scroll event loop pattern.

IntersectionObserver options: root (default null = viewport), rootMargin (CSS-style margins for early/late triggering), scrollMargin (margins around nested scroll containers), threshold (single number or array of intersection ratios 0.0-1.0, default 0), delay (min 100ms when trackVisibility is true), trackVisibility (boolean, expensive, requires delay). For fade-in: threshold 0.1, rootMargin '0px 0px -10% 0px' to trigger slightly before fully visible.

IntersectionObserver callback runs on the main thread and 'should operate as quickly as possible.' Best practice: add or remove CSS classes on the target element (let the browser handle the visual transition via CSS). Do NOT trigger heavy JS or layout calculations in the callback.

Chrome 116+ supports animating display and content-visibility in keyframes. 'display' and 'content-visibility' switch states at the beginning (in) or end (out) of the transition. This unlocks entry/exit animations including from display: none.

@starting-style is now in Baseline (2024). 'The @starting-style rule defines the initial styles on an element before it's rendered on the page. This is required for elements that animate in.' Caveat: @starting-style rules must be placed AFTER post-entry state declarations (Chrome 130+).

transition-behavior: allow-discrete (Chrome 117+) enables transitions of discrete properties like display. Combined with @starting-style, this unlocks full entry/exit animations on popovers, dialogs, and conditional-rendered DOM nodes. For v1 we don't need this — we render once and animate, not toggle visibility.

Gwern.net 'Design Of This Website': 4 design principles — aesthetically-pleasing minimalism, accessibility/progressive-enhancement, speed, and 'semantic zoom' (progressive disclosure as the reader requests more). 'JavaScript is not required for the core reading experience, only for (mostly) optional features: popups & transclusions, table-sorting, sidenotes.' 'Festina lente: speed is the 2nd-most important feature after correctness.' Pandoc + Hakyll, no client-side JS for the readable surface.

Gwern's 'semantic zoom' concept: a single page can be read at multiple structural levels (title, metadata, abstract, headers, margin notes, body, footnotes, collapsed sections, fulltext links) with reader-driven progressive disclosure. This is the philosophical underpinning of how a long dossier should be presented — and it maps directly to HTML <details>/<summary>, markdown-it-container collapse, and IntersectionObserver fade-in as the reader scrolls.

Gwern's design inspirations: English Wikipedia, Project Xanadu, Tufte, Robert Bringhurst, Christopher Alexander, Susan Kare, Donald Knuth, Markdeep, Andy Matuschak evergreen notes, Dieter Rams, Jakob Nielsen. Andre's fleet already has Obsidian (which shares DNA with Andy Matuschak's evergreen notes model), so the dossier rendering is the consumption-side complement to the authoring-side Obsidian experience.

Tufte CSS (Dave Liepmann, edwardtufte/tufte-css). Sidenotes (margin notes that don't force reader to jump to bottom of page), full-width figures, careful typography. h1 title, h2 section, h3 low-level. Quote (Edward Tufte): 'The Feynman lectures use only 2 levels of hierarchical headings: chapters and A-level heads in the text.' Cap heading depth at 3.

Tufte CSS colors: #fffff8 off-white, #111111 off-black. ETBook serif font with Palatino/Georgia fallback. 'The web is not print. Webpages are not books. The goal of Tufte CSS is not to say websites should look like this interpretation of Tufte's books but rather here are some techniques Tufte developed that we've found useful in print; maybe you can find a way to make them useful on the web.' This is the right framing for the dossier: borrow the techniques, don't copy the look.

Tufte CSS uses a 'newthought' span (class) for the first few words of a section in small caps. Vertical spacing accomplished through <section> tags. 'The goal is to make sentences readable without interference from links.' For Mavis's dossier: borrow sidenotes sparingly, use the 'first words in small caps' for new sections, and don't compete with the prose.

text-wrap: pretty (Chrome 117+). 'text-wrap: pretty is for generally better line wrapping and text breaking, currently focused on orphans. In the future, text-wrap: pretty may offer more behavior.' Pair with text-wrap: balance for headings.

text-wrap: balance vs text-wrap: pretty: balance is for headings and short blocks (line lengths equalized); pretty is for paragraphs (orphans avoided on last line). Cross-browser: Chrome 117+, WebKit/Safari 17.4+, Firefox 121+. Cross-referenced with WebKit blog for independent confirmation.
WebKit blog independently confirms text-wrap: pretty implementation, providing cross-vendor verification of the feature's availability and behavior in Safari.

HTTP Archive Web Almanac 2025 Page Weight chapter (Jan 15, 2026). Median desktop home page in 2025: 2.86MB (HTML 22KB, CSS 82KB, fonts 139KB, JS 697KB, images 1058KB). Median mobile home page: 2.56MB. YoY growth: +7.8% desktop, +8.4% mobile. The web is heavier every year.

90th percentile desktop home page is 9.18MB; 90th percentile mobile is 8.34MB. JavaScript median 697KB (desktop) / 632KB (mobile). 100KB of JavaScript is computationally heavy; an image is just pixels. The JavaScript tax is the most expensive part of a modern page.

HTTP Archive 2025 on AI crawlers: 'All key information should be present in the initial raw HTML, since AI crawlers do not render JavaScript.' This is a strong argument for the self-contained single-file pattern — it's not just a performance win, it's a content accessibility win for the AI-crawler era.

Web Vitals canonical (web.dev). Core Web Vitals (all Stable as of 2024): LCP < 2.5s (loading performance), INP <= 200ms (interactivity), CLS <= 0.1 (visual stability). 75th percentile of real-user measurements is the assessment target.
INP (Interaction to Next Paint) replaced FID as a Stable Core Web Vital in 2024. INP measures all interactions on the page, not just the first input. Lower is better; 200ms is the 'Good' threshold.

web-vitals JS library: `import {onCLS, onINP, onLCP} from 'web-vitals'` is the standard way to measure all three Core Web Vitals in production. v2 task: wire Mavis's render pipeline to call this on page load, send the data to a Mavis-managed RUM endpoint, set up a `routes/RUM/*` consumer.

web-platform-dx features registry confirms scroll-driven-animations is 'baseline: false' as of 2026-06. Chrome 115+ supports it; Firefox and Safari do not (Safari 26 has some but not all of the spec). This is the basis for the progressive-enhancement fallback plan: IntersectionObserver for all browsers, scroll-driven animations for Chrome 115+ and Safari 26+ via @supports.

Claude Artifacts (Anthropic, Jun 21, 2024, with Claude 3.5 Sonnet). 'Artifacts on Claude.ai, a new feature that expands how users can interact with Claude. When a user asks Claude to generate content like code snippets, text documents, or website designs, these Artifacts appear in a dedicated window alongside their conversation. This creates a dynamic workspace where they can see, edit, and build upon Claude's creations in real-time. This preview feature marks Claude's evolution from a conversational AI to a collaborative work environment.' Made generally available Aug 28, 2024.

The Claude Artifacts pattern — AI generates long, structured content, the product surfaces it in a dedicated panel for the human to read/interact with — is the leading edge of a pattern that every major AI lab is converging on. ChatGPT Canvas (OpenAI, Oct 2024), Perplexity Pages (May 2024), Google Gemini Gems all solve the same problem. Mavis's vault HTML is the Mavis-archive instance of this pattern.

Claude 3.5 Sonnet: $3/M input, $15/M output, 200K context, ASL-2 safety. 200K context window is enough for Mavis's typical dossier (3,000-8,000 words ~= 4,000-11,000 tokens); the dossier fits comfortably in a single context window for any Claude model.

Perplexity Pages (May 30, 2024). 'Meet Perplexity Pages, your new tool for easily transforming research into visually stunning, comprehensive content.' Features: quick content generation, highly customizable, media insertion, source validation and citation, SEO-optimized. Initial Pro users, then rolled out to all users. The 'AI Wikipedia' framing.

The AI-output-to-landing-page pattern is convergent: Anthropic (Artifacts), OpenAI (Canvas), Perplexity (Pages), Google (Gems). All launched 2024-2025. All surface AI-generated long-form content in a dedicated, often side-paneled, often shareable page. This is the pattern Mavis is implementing for her own work — the markdown dossier IS the artifact, the HTML is the consumption surface.

Anthropic engineer Thariq's viral 'MD is dead, long live HTML' thread (May 12, 2026, reported by Tencent News, 36kr, Sohu, Pengpai, IT之家, NetEase, DoNews). Five-point argument: (1) HTML has higher information density (tables, CSS, SVG, JS, canvas). (2) MD over 100 lines is unreadable. (3) HTML5 brings back authoring control. (4) AI agents output HTML more naturally. (5) HTML closes the AI-human authoring gap. Karpathy endorsed. Secondary source — original Thariq thread on X not directly fetched.

Implication of Thariq's thread for our pipeline: long-form AI outputs may converge to HTML as the authoring format, not Markdown. Our rendering layer (markdown-it → standalone HTML) is on the leading edge of this trend. The source of truth is still markdown (it's the right authoring surface for Mavis in Obsidian); the rendered artifact is HTML. Watch for corroboration from other AI labs.

Mavis skill: content-deck-generator v1.0.0. Reads ~/.mavis/fleet/*-draft.md files, parses X (tweet thread) and LinkedIn (longform) buckets, renders standalone dark-theme HTML deck with numbered tweet cards and LinkedIn block, Copy buttons, 60s auto-refresh via <meta http-equiv='refresh' content='60'> + inline countdown. Output: ~/.mavis/fleet/outbound/content-deck.html.

content-deck-generator is the closest existing pattern in Mavis's catalog. Borrow the auto-refresh + standalone-file pattern for the fleet-status surface; the dark theme is a per-skill choice (the dossier surface should be light/auto by default, not forced dark).

Mavis skill: html-presentation-generator. Multi-page HTML presentations exportable to PDF/PPTX. Fixed 960x540 dimensions per slide. Times New Roman. All CSS inline. 'No animations: No CSS animations, transitions, hover effects, or SVG animations.' Anti-animation rule is a feature for export-to-binary, not for reading.
html-presentation-generator produces binary-exportable slide decks, not animated long-form reading. The shape is wrong for our use case. Ignore for v1.

Mavis skill: landing-page-builder. Awwwards-level landing pages with cinematic hero, deployed online. Spec-first workflow. Video-First hero. The 'spec.md' discipline is the right model for our `01 Build Spec.md`; the deliverable shape is wrong (hero-driven, not reading-driven; deployed to a public URL, not vault-resident).

landing-page-builder's spec-first discipline (Step 2: write spec.md with expanded prompt, tech strategy, design system) is the right shape for our v1 build spec. The v1 build will produce a 200-line Node script, not a public landing page, but the spec discipline transfers.

View Transitions API. Same-document view transitions supported Chrome 111+. Cross-document supported Chrome 126+. Pattern: document.startViewTransition(() => { DOM mutation }). Pseudo-elements for snapshot management. view-transition-name is the CSS property. For v1, defer — we render one long page, not navigate between pages.
View Transitions cross-document (Chrome 126+) is the right primitive if/when the dossier/brief/fleet-status become separate pages rather than one long page. v2 question.

@starting-style + transition-behavior: allow-discrete (Chrome 117+, Baseline 2024). Enables entry/exit animations for new DOM nodes and from display: none. Caveat: @starting-style rules must be placed AFTER post-entry state declarations (Chrome 130+). For v1: not needed; we render once and animate via @keyframes + IntersectionObserver.

Web Animations API is the framework underneath scroll-driven animations, View Transitions, and CSS @keyframes. Element.animate() method is the JS equivalent of @keyframes + animation properties. For dossier: not needed directly; we use CSS @keyframes only.

Mavis skill: visual-summary. Trigger: 'visual summary', 'visualize', 'animate', 'demonstrate', 'interactive demo', 'principle', '原理'. Currently NOT installed at the standard path. The trigger logic is the right precedent; our dossier rendering is the broader generalization of this trigger to long-form text outputs.

web.dev: 'Now in Baseline: animating entry effects' (2024). Independent confirmation of @starting-style and transition-behavior: allow-discrete as cross-browser supported primitives. Cross-references src-2026-06-04-015 (Chrome DevRel) and src-2026-06-04-014 (MDN).

W3C CSSWG Scroll-driven Animations spec (drafts.csswg.org/scroll-animations-1) is the canonical specification. Cross-references MDN, Chrome DevRel, and web-platform-dx for independent confirmation of the property names and semantics. Spec defines scroll-timeline-name, scroll-timeline-axis, view-timeline-name, view-timeline-axis, view-timeline-inset, animation-range-start, animation-range-end, animation-range shorthand, scroll() function, view() function.

CommonMark Spec 0.31 is the underlying specification for GFM and most modern markdown parsers. Created by John MacFarlane (jgm), 2014 onwards. 600+ conformance tests in the spec test suite. Both CommonMark and GFM should be supported by the v1 renderer (markdown-it handles both).

Federal Register notice 91 FR 20465 (Doc. 2026-07361, published April 16, 2026, Docket No. FDA-2025-N-6895) establishes the PCAC meeting on July 23-24, 2026, opens a public comment docket (close July 22, 2026), and lists the 7-peptide agenda across 2 days.

PCAC meeting location: FDA White Oak Campus, 10903 New Hampshire Ave., Bldg. 31 Conference Center, the Great Room (Rm. 1503), Silver Spring, MD 20993-0002. Online teleconference/video conference participation available.

July 23, 2026 agenda (4 peptides, all considered for 503A Bulks List inclusion): BPC-157 (free base) / BPC-157 acetate — ulcerative colitis (UC); KPV (free base) / KPV acetate — wound healing and inflammatory conditions; TB-500 / TB-500 acetate — wound healing; MOTs-C / MOTs-C acetate — obesity and osteoporosis. Uses evaluated column from FR notice matches FDA meeting page.

July 24, 2026 agenda (3 peptides, all considered for 503A Bulks List inclusion): Emideltide (also referred to as delta sleeping inducing peptide / DSIP) (free base) / Emideltide acetate — opioid withdrawal, chronic insomnia, and narcolepsy; Semax (free base) / Semax acetate — cerebral ischemia, migraine, and trigeminal neuralgia; Epitalon (free base) / Epitalon acetate — insomnia. Uses evaluated column from FR notice matches FDA meeting page.

The 7 peptides are being considered for INCLUSION on the 503A Bulks List (the legal compounding gateway under section 503A), not for placement in Category 1 and not for re-placement in any other interim-policy category. Confirmed verbatim by both the Federal Register notice ('the Committee will discuss the following bulk drug substances being considered for inclusion on the 503A Bulks List') and the FDA 503A bulks hub page (which describes the 3-category interim policy framework).

FDA's interim enforcement discretion policy applies ONLY to Category 1 substances. Per FDA's official 503A bulks page: 'FDA does not intend to take action against a compounder for compounding drugs using bulk drug substances listed in category 1, provided that the conditions described in the guidance document are met.' Category 2 substances are explicitly NOT in scope of the policy. Removal from Category 2 does not by itself place a substance in Category 1.

12 peptide bulk drug substances scheduled for Category 2 removal within 7 calendar days of April 15, 2026 (effective approximately April 22, 2026). Full enumeration (both law-firm sources concur): BPC-157, Cathelicidin LL-37, Dihexa Acetate, Emideltide (DSIP), Epitalon, GHK-Cu (for injectable routes of administration), KPV, Mechano Growth Factor Pegylated (PEG-MGF), Melanotan II, MOTs-C, Semax (heptapeptide), Thymosin Beta-4 Fragment (LKKTETQ) (TB-500). All 12 removals driven by nomination withdrawal, not by FDA safety reassessment.

A second PCAC meeting is scheduled before end of February 2027 to consider the remaining 5 peptides (Cathelicidin LL-37, Dihexa Acetate, GHK-Cu injectable, PEG-MGF, Melanotan II), plus GHK-Cu non-injectable (previously in Category 1, also slated for the second meeting).

Procedural status between April 22, 2026 (Category 2 removal effective) and the PCAC vote(s): the 12 peptides are in a 'regulatory gray area' — outside Category 2 (no longer flagged with 'significant safety risks' designation), not in Category 1 (no enforcement discretion), and not on the 503A bulks list. FDA has not (as of 2026-06-05) extended enforcement discretion to any of the 12. Per Orrick: 'Stakeholders should closely monitor this process but should not begin compounding with any of the 12 peptides solely on the basis of their removal from Category 2.'

Section 503A conditions (always required, regardless of any future 503A Bulks List placement): bulk drug substance must (a) comply with an applicable USP/NF monograph if one exists AND the USP chapter on pharmacy compounding, OR (b) be a component of an FDA-approved drug product if monograph does not exist, OR (c) appear on the 503A bulks list. Additionally: valid certificate of analysis, manufacturer registered with FDA under section 510 of the FD&C Act, compounding pursuant to a valid prescription for an individually identified patient.

Prior 2024 PCAC precedent: voted against inclusion of all previously reviewed peptides, finding unacceptable safety risks. PCAC currently lists only 4 members; several prior members removed and not yet replaced; committee 'does not presently appear to have a quorum, a procedural issue that could affect its ability to act at the scheduled meeting.' Per Orrick: 'the current environment is materially different: many prior FDA staff have departed, the PCAC has significant vacancies that Secretary Kennedy could fill before the July meeting, and the administration has publicly signaled support for broader peptide access.'

M3 ships with 1M-token context window via MSA (MiniMax Sparse Attention) and is the first open-weights model to combine frontier coding + 1M context + native multimodality + desktop computer use in a single release.
M3 single-token compute at 1M context is ~1/20th of the prior generation. New data-read and compute-path redesign yields 4x+ performance vs mainstream open-source solutions on long-context tasks.
M3 is MoE: 196B total parameters with only 11B active per token. 400 TPS single-request throughput, compatible with Claude Code and MCP protocol, supports both cloud and local deployment.

Claude Code's macOS computer use is a built-in MCP server, activated via /mcp, no Docker/VM required. Tool selection priority: MCP > shell > Chrome extension > computer use last-resort. Anthropic ships Desktop Extensions (.dxt) for one-click MCP install.

Claude Desktop's filesystem MCP server exposes a 3-click install pattern: pick a path, the model can read/write/rename/move files. Obsidian's vault is a plain folder of Markdown files — the same MCP works without an Obsidian-specific server.

MCP donated to Linux Foundation 2025-12; >97M monthly downloads by 2026-04 and >110M by mid-2026 (Anthropic's David Soria Parra). 4000+ registered MCP servers. Three-layer production connection stack: Skills (domain knowledge in Markdown) + CLI/Computer Use (local execution, ~200 tokens/response) + MCP (connectivity with OAuth/governance/audit).

CLI agents are 10-32x cheaper in tokens and near-100% reliable vs MCP agents at 72% reliability. Anthropic internal research: shell scripts vs MCP tools = 98.7% token reduction. Three MCP servers = 150K token overhead before the task starts. Perplexity publicly removed MCP from their agent for the same reason.

OSWorld-MCP benchmark (Jia et al. Oct 2025): agents using MCP tools score higher than pure-GUI agents on real desktop workflows. Stanford HAI 2026 AI Index: OSWorld AI agent success rate rose from 12% (2025) to 66.3% (2026-03); human baseline is 72-74%. The hybrid vision+tool harness dominates pure-vision or pure-MCP.

Hierarchical file-based memory with Adaptive Knowledge Lifecycle (AKL): Context Tree (arXiv 2604.01599) + Letta Memory Blocks + MemGPT recursive summarization. The convergence: page in entire files as memory blocks; the LLM decides what to read/write/pin.

Token-budgeted RAG is a 2026 research frontier: AdaGReS (Dec 2025) uses redundancy-aware greedy selection; TeaRAG (Nov 2025) targets token efficiency in agentic RAG. The pattern: budget the chunks by marginal gain, not by similarity score.

Meta Superintelligence Lab's REFRAG compresses low-relevance chunks via embeddings while preserving key chunks' original tokens: 30.85x TTFT speedup, 16x context length extension. The asymmetric-compression pattern: preserve the signal, compress the noise.

Context caching (Moonshot, Anthropic prompt caching) yields 90% cost reduction and 83% first-token latency reduction for repeated large-doc QA. Cache stable for 10 minutes. The pattern: stable prompt prefix = the cache key. Compress-and-cache + anchor-attention for stable prefix is the production trick.

PagedEviction (arXiv 2509.04377) drops the lowest-importance blocks by ||V||/||K|| ratio. 10-12% lower latency, 15-20% better accuracy than StreamingLLM. Combined with AKL's importance scoring: importance-aware eviction is the durable paging strategy.

Harness engineering moved LangChain's coding agent from outside top 30 to rank 5 on Terminal Bench 2.0 (same model). OpenAI disclosed Codex built a 1M+ line production app with zero human-written code. Martin Fowler framing: Agent = Model + Harness. The harness is where the hard engineering lives.

The 2026 industry convergence: the harness is the primary tool for solving model drift (Phil Schmid). Self-evolution via observability-driven harness optimization (Fudan AHE) added 7.3 points on Terminal-Bench 2.0. Eval harness as production gate (golden tests + CI + drift detection).

Three-tier intent-routing funnel is the 2026 industry default: L1 regex intercepts ~80% of fixed commands at zero latency; L2 vector-semantic handles paraphrases; L3 LLM classifier for natural language. The pattern: deterministic-first, LLM-last.

Long-context RAG convergence: even with 1M context windows, intelligent routing + KV caching + paged memory are required. Pure long-context replaces naive RAG, but agentic RAG (tool-using) still beats it on multi-document and dynamic-data tasks. The pattern: long-context + selective RAG, not long-context OR RAG.

Mac-native LLM client + desktop-app presence is the load-bearing path for the 2026 chief-of-staff stack. Standard chat API friction: per-request token overhead, session-state fragmentation, no on-device FS. Mac-native path: filesystem MCP (3 clicks), computer use (built-in MCP), no Docker, no VM, in-process state.
